prior.content_md:
*This section describes the rule's requirements as they apply to vehicle manufacturers — the direct compliance subjects — and translates the operational implications for the workspace as a purchaser of carrier services.*

## Standards Structure

The Phase 3 rule sets GHG emissions standards for:
- Heavy-duty vocational vehicles (Classes 2b–8 vocational applications)
- Tractors (day cabs and sleeper cabs, Classes 7–8)

Standards apply beginning MY 2027 and become more stringent through MY 2032.

*Source: EPA, "Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Commercial Trucks & Buses," last updated February 13, 2026.*
*Source: Federal Register 89 FR 29440, April 22, 2024.*

## Relationship to Prior Phases

Phase 1 (MY 2014–2018) and Phase 2 (MY 2019–2027) established the baseline. Phase 3 builds on both. Carriers currently operating MY 2019–2026 compliant fleets are operating under Phase 2 standards; their equipment does not need to be replaced immediately, but new purchases beginning MY 2027 must meet Phase 3 standards.

*Source: EPA, "Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Commercial Trucks & Buses," last updated February 13, 2026.*

## What "More Stringent" Means Operationally for Carrier Fleets

The rule requires manufacturers to produce vehicles with lower GHG emissions per ton-mile or per vehicle, which in practice means a shift toward ZEV drivetrains (battery electric, fuel cell) or substantially improved internal combustion efficiency for the heaviest classes. The extent to which each vehicle category must achieve ZEV-level compliance versus advanced ICE compliance, and the specific numerical standards by class and application, are set out in the Federal Register at 89 FR 29440. *Specific numerical emission rate targets by vehicle class are available in the Federal Register record; the workspace should obtain the full rule text for reference in carrier conversations.*

*Source: Federal Register 89 FR 29440, April 22, 2024. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/04/22/2024-06809/greenhouse-gas-emissions-standards-for-heavy-duty-vehicles-phase-3*

## ZEV Infrastructure Dependency

The 2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy (published jointly by the U.S. DOE, DOT, and EPA) identifies charging and hydrogen fueling infrastructure availability as a binding constraint on heavy-duty ZEV adoption timelines, particularly for long-haul Class 8 operations. This means Phase 3 compliance by carriers on long-haul corridors (relevant to high-value automotive and humanitarian cargo) depends not only on vehicle availability but on corridor-by-corridor infrastructure build-out, which is not guaranteed on all routes the workspace uses.

*Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, "2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy" (DOE/EE-2816), 2024. https://driveelectric.gov/files/zef-corridor-strategy.pdf*

---
